2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Takabe Shrine | 画像=[[File:Takabe jinja haiden.JPG|260px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}{{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}} Minamiasai 164 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|57|44|N|139|56|58|E|display=it}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 神事=Hōchō ritual | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Takabe Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabe Shrine''' (たかべじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}} (former {{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}}). It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is revered by people in culinary professions and soy sauce brewers as a shrine enshrining the “ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=cooking|Q1778821|WD=Q1778821|1=Cuisine}}.” ===Enshrined deities=== It enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takabe-no-Kami), and also {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|lt=Inari|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}. ===History=== The origin of this shrine’s foundation is unknown, but the shrine’s history states that some descendants of the {{ill|lt=Takahashi clan|Q11671376|WD=Q11671376|1=Takahashi}}, descendants of Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto, may have moved to Awa Province, which was connected to their ancestral deity, and enshrined that deity as their ujigami. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Takabe Shrine, {{ill|lt=Asai District|Q4803406|WD=Q4803406|1=Asai District, Chiba}}, Awa Province,” but it appears to have later declined and disappeared, and its location remained unknown for a long time. The origin of the current Takabe Shrine dates to the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. About 200 years later, it was found that one mirror bore the inscription “Miketsu no Kami, Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto,” and it was judged to be the sacred object of the long-lost Takabe Shrine. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:南房総市の歴史]] [[Category:南房総市の建築物]] [[Category:安房国の式内社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:日本の食文化|神]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:房総の魅力500選]]

2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Takabe Shrine | 画像=[[File:Takabe jinja haiden.JPG|260px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}{{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}} Minamiasai 164 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|57|44|N|139|56|58|E|display=it}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 神事=Hōchō ritual | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Takabe Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabe Shrine''' (たかべじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}} (former {{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}}). It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is revered by people in culinary professions and soy sauce brewers as a shrine enshrining the “ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=cooking|Q1778821|WD=Q1778821|1=Cuisine}}.” ===Enshrined deities=== It enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takabe-no-Kami), and also {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|lt=Inari|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}. ===History=== The origin of this shrine’s foundation is unknown, but the shrine’s history states that some descendants of the {{ill|lt=Takahashi clan|Q11671376|WD=Q11671376|1=Takahashi}}, descendants of Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto, may have moved to Awa Province, which was connected to their ancestral deity, and enshrined that deity as their ujigami. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Takabe Shrine, {{ill|lt=Asai District|Q4803406|WD=Q4803406|1=Asai District, Chiba}}, Awa Province,” but it appears to have later declined and disappeared, and its location remained unknown for a long time. The origin of the current Takabe Shrine dates to the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. About 200 years later, it was found that one mirror bore the inscription “Miketsu no Kami, Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto,” and it was judged to be the sacred object of the long-lost Takabe Shrine. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:南房総市の歴史]] [[Category:南房総市の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:日本の食文化|神]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:房総の魅力500選]]

== street address (P6375) == * {'text': '千葉県南房総市千倉町南朝夷164', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:高家神社]] [[SV:Takabe-jinja]] [[ZH:高家神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11669186}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記|date=February 2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Takabe Shrine | 画像=[[File:Takabe jinja haiden.JPG|260px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}{{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}} Minamiasai 164 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|57|44|N|139|56|58|E|display=it}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 神事=Hōchō ritual | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Takabe Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabe Shrine''' (たかべじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}} (former {{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}}). It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is revered by people in culinary professions and soy sauce brewers as a shrine enshrining the “ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=cooking|Q1778821|WD=Q1778821|1=Cuisine}}.” ===Enshrined deities=== It enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takabe-no-Kami), and also {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|lt=Inari|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}. ===History=== The origin of this shrine’s foundation is unknown, but the shrine’s history states that some descendants of the {{ill|lt=Takahashi clan|Q11671376|WD=Q11671376|1=Takahashi}}, descendants of Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto, may have moved to Awa Province, which was connected to their ancestral deity, and enshrined that deity as their ujigami. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Takabe Shrine, {{ill|lt=Asai District|Q4803406|WD=Q4803406|1=Asai District, Chiba}}, Awa Province,” but it appears to have later declined and disappeared, and its location remained unknown for a long time. The origin of the current Takabe Shrine dates to the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. About 200 years later, it was found that one mirror bore the inscription “Miketsu no Kami, Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto,” and it was judged to be the sacred object of the long-lost Takabe Shrine. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:南房総市の歴史]] [[Category:南房総市の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese cuisine]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:Top 500 Attractions of Bōsō Peninsula]]

In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:南房総市の歴史]] [[Category:南房総市の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese cuisine]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:Top 500 Attractions of Bōsō Peninsula]]

2020}} {{神社 | 名称=Takabe Shrine | 画像=[[File:Takabe jinja haiden.JPG|260px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}{{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}} Minamiasai 164 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|57|44|N|139|56|58|E|display=it}} | 祭神={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 神事=Hōchō ritual | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Takabe Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabe Shrine''' (たかべじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}} (former {{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}}). It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is revered by people in culinary professions and soy sauce brewers as a shrine enshrining the “ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=cooking|Q1778821|WD=Q1778821|1=Cuisine}}.” ===Enshrined deities=== It enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takabe-no-Kami), and also {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|lt=Inari|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}. ===History=== The origin of this shrine’s foundation is unknown, but the shrine’s history states that some descendants of the {{ill|lt=Takahashi clan|Q11671376|WD=Q11671376|1=Takahashi}}, descendants of Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto, may have moved to Awa Province, which was connected to their ancestral deity, and enshrined that deity as their ujigami. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Takabe Shrine, {{ill|lt=Asai District|Q4803406|WD=Q4803406|1=Asai District, Chiba}}, Awa Province,” but it appears to have later declined and disappeared, and its location remained unknown for a long time. The origin of the current Takabe Shrine dates to the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. About 200 years later, it was found that one mirror bore the inscription “Miketsu no Kami, Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto,” and it was judged to be the sacred object of the long-lost Takabe Shrine. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:南房総市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Minamiboso]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese cuisine]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:Top 500 Attractions of Bōsō Peninsula]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=UNKNOWN}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 神事=Hōchō ritual | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=Takabe Shrine|position=right|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabe Shrine''' (たかべじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Minamibōsō|Q873521|WD=Q873521|1=Minamibōsō}}, {{ill|lt=Chiba Prefecture|Q80011|WD=Q80011|1=Chiba Prefecture}} (former {{ill|lt=Chikura Town|Q5097510|WD=Q5097510|1=Chikura, Chiba}}). It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is revered by people in culinary professions and soy sauce brewers as a shrine enshrining the “ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=cooking|Q1778821|WD=Q1778821|1=Cuisine}}.” ===Enshrined deities=== It enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takabe-no-Kami), and also {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu-Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|lt=Inari|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}}. ===History=== The origin of this shrine’s foundation is unknown, but the shrine’s history states that some descendants of the {{ill|lt=Takahashi clan|Q11671376|WD=Q11671376|1=Takahashi}}, descendants of Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto, may have moved to Awa Province, which was connected to their ancestral deity, and enshrined that deity as their ujigami. It is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Takabe Shrine, {{ill|lt=Asai District|Q4803406|WD=Q4803406|1=Asai District, Chiba}}, Awa Province,” but it appears to have later declined and disappeared, and its location remained unknown for a long time. The origin of the current Takabe Shrine dates to the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. In {{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1620|Q6810|WD=Q6810|1=1620}}), Takagi Kichiemon discovered a wooden statue and two mirrors beneath a cherry tree, and founded a shrine called “Shinmei-sha,” using them as sacred objects. About 200 years later, it was found that one mirror bore the inscription “Miketsu no Kami, Iwakamutsukari-no-Mikoto,” and it was judged to be the sacred object of the long-lost Takabe Shrine. In {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1819|Q6967|WD=Q6967|1=1819}}), a petition was submitted to the Yoshida office in Kyoto for official recognition, and the shrine was renamed from Shinmei-sha to Takabe Shrine. A divided spirit of this shrine is enshrined at the {{ill|lt=Higeta Shōyu|Q11329982|WD=Q11329982|1=Higeta Shoyu}} factory in {{ill|lt=Chōshi|Q694317|WD=Q694317|1=Chōshi}} as a guardian deity of soy sauce brewing. It was enshrined there by Tanaka Naotarō (Kinchōshi), then president of the company and later author of ''Shōyu Enkakushi''. Through introductions by Tanaka Naotarō, Ishii Jibei (author of ''Nihon Ryōrihō Taizen''), and Miyake Koken of the Japanese Cuisine Research Society, the name of Takabe Shrine became known throughout Japan. === Notes === {{Reflist}} ===External links=== * [https://takabejinja.com/ Official Takabe Shrine website] == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takabe Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Minamiboso]] [[Category:Buildings in Minamiboso]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Awa Province (Chiba)]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese cuisine]] [[Category:高橋氏|社たかへしんしや]] [[Category:Top 500 Attractions of Bōsō Peninsula]]
